description: This figure presents optical micrographs and corresponding schematic illustrations comparing the microstructures of fully austenitic and austenite-based duplex Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels. Panel (a) shows a fully austenitic steel exhibiting equiaxed austenite grains with abundant annealing twins, indicating a fully recovered microstructure (scale bar: 50 μm). Panel (b) displays an austenite-based duplex steel (γ matrix) containing elongated ferrite grains (δ) aligned along the rolling direction (RD), as indicated by the RD/ND coordinate system (scale bar: 20 μm). Panels (c) and (d) provide schematic representations of the fully austenite and duplex microstructures, respectively, clearly delineating annealing twins within austenite grains and the distribution of ferrite grains within the austenite matrix. These images demonstrate that through controlled alloying and heat treatment, the material can develop either single-phase austenitic microstructures characterized by annealing twins or duplex microstructures with ferrite elongated along the rolling direction, establishing the microstructural foundation for mechanical property optimization in these lightweight structural steels.

This figure presents a series of three isothermal phase section diagrams (subfigures a, b, c) illustrating the phase equilibria of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels at 900°C with varying manganese contents. Subfigure (a) shows the phase diagram at 10 wt% Mn, (b) at 20 wt% Mn, and (c) at 30 wt% Mn, all plotted with aluminum content (0-14 wt%) on the x-axis and carbon content (0-4 wt%) on the y-axis. The diagrams are constructed based on both experimental data (represented by discrete symbols including circles, squares, and triangles) and CALPHAD thermodynamic calculations (solid and dashed boundary lines). The color-coded phase regions identify austenite (γ, light gray), ferrite (α, gold/yellow), and κ-carbide (blue), along with multi-phase fields such as γ+M₃C, γ+κ, γ+α, and α+κ. The figure demonstrates that increasing Mn content from 10% to 30% significantly expands the single-phase austenite region toward higher aluminum concentrations (>8 wt%) and enlarges the κ-phase stability field at high carbon (>3 wt%) and high aluminum (>10 wt%) contents. Additionally, the diagrams reveal the emergence of M₃C and κ phases when carbon exceeds 1.2 wt%, and the expansion of the ferrite (α) phase region with increasing aluminum content in the 20-30 wt% Mn alloys. These 900°C isothermal sections are critical for alloy design as this temperature approximates industrial hot rolling conditions, guiding the optimization of Mn, Al, and C contents to achieve desired microstructures (single austenite vs. duplex) and prevent detrimental phase formation during processing.

This time-temperature-transformation (TTT) diagram illustrates the decomposition pathway of the γ (austenite) phase in Fe–28Mn–8.5Al–1C–1.2Si low-density steel during aging treatment. The plot features temperature (°C) on the vertical axis and time (hours) on a logarithmic horizontal axis spanning 0.01 to 1000 hours. Two distinct cooling regimes are delineated by shaded regions: 'Fast cooling' (left side, steep cooling trajectory) and 'Slow cooling' (right side), separated by a critical cooling rate of -4.4 °C/h indicated by a dashed line. Under fast cooling conditions, the austenite transforms sequentially from single-phase γ (region 1) to γ+κ' (region 2). Conversely, slow cooling promotes the formation of equilibrium κ* precipitates and subsequent decomposition products including α (B2/DO₃) ordered phases. The diagram identifies five distinct phase fields: (1) γ; (2) γ+κ'; (3) γ+κ*; (4) γ+κ*+α(B2/DO₃); and (5) γ+κ+B2/DO₃, with the right-hand axis indicating temperature ranges for B2, B2+DO₃, and DO₃ phase stability. The figure demonstrates that κ-carbide precipitation from the austenite matrix occurs when the cooling rate is slower than -4.4 °C/h, providing a predictive framework for controlling austenite decomposition and κ precipitation through cooling rate management to optimize mechanical properties.

This figure illustrates the twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) mechanism and its effect on work hardening in Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels. Panels (a) and (b) are schematic diagrams showing dislocation interactions with deformation twins: (a) primary twins divide austenite grains with dislocations pinned at twin boundaries, while (b) secondary twins further subdivide the matrix. Panels (c) and (d) are TEM micrographs revealing deformation twin morphologies; (c) shows Twin-ε with a SAED pattern (inset), scale bar 500 nm, and (d) shows intersecting twins with scale bar 200 nm. Panel (e) plots the F/T ratio (fraction of twinned grains to twin thickness) versus true strain, comparing FeMnC (high ratio, strong work hardening) and FeMnCAl (suppressed twinning). Panel (f) shows true stress and work-hardening rate curves for 0Al and 1.5Al steels, indicating Al reduces work hardening. The figure demonstrates that Al addition increases stacking fault energy, inhibiting twinning and dynamic Hall-Petch strengthening.

This figure presents the work hardening behavior and tensile flow stress of individual phases in Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel, comprising three panels adapted from Dan et al. Panels (a) and (b) display the evolution of work hardening rate (open symbols) and flow stress (filled symbols) versus effective plastic strain for ferrite, bainite, retained austenite, and martensite phases, extracted using ABAQUS/UMAT framework. Critical necking strains are marked: bainite at 0.127, ferrite at 0.163, austenite at 0.348, and martensite at 0.50. Panel (c) illustrates the Considere criterion, indicating that necking initiates when the work hardening rate equals the true stress. The data demonstrate that martensite exhibits the highest hardening rate and necking strain (0.50), significantly exceeding those of austenite, ferrite, and bainite. This supports the TRIP effect mechanism where retained austenite transforms to martensite during deformation, providing sustained work hardening capacity that delays necking and enhances uniform elongation and tensile strength in duplex austenite-based steels.

This multi-panel figure illustrates the transition from wavy to planar dislocation slip in Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels with increasing aluminum content and its effect on work hardening. Panel (a) plots the parameter $W_R$ (×10⁻⁴ m) versus Al content (wt.%), showing a linear decrease from approximately 29 at 0% Al to 10 at 6% Al, which predicts the slip mode transition. Subfigures (a1) and (a2) are TEM micrographs (scale bar 200 nm) comparing Fe-22Mn-3Al and Fe-22Mn-6Al alloys strained to ε=0.1, revealing a change from dislocation cells (DCs) characteristic of wavy slip to planar slip bands. Panel (b) presents an ECCI micrograph (scale bar 1 μm) showing narrow, dense coplanar slip microbands at ε=0.14. Panel (c) displays true stress and strain hardening rate curves versus true strain, demonstrating the SRO-enhanced work hardening behavior. Collectively, the figure provides evidence that Al addition promotes short-range order (SRO), facilitating planar slip and microband formation, which significantly contributes to the work hardening capacity of these steels.

This 3D surface plot illustrates the calculated relationship between yield strength improvement (Δσ_P) and carbide precipitation parameters in Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel based on the Ashby-Orowan strengthening model. The X-axis represents carbide size (0–100 nm), the Y-axis represents volume fraction f (0–1.0%), and the Z-axis represents yield strength increment Δσ_P (0–900 MPa). The colored curved surface demonstrates that higher volume fractions and smaller particle sizes significantly enhance strengthening effects. Experimental data points for specific carbides are marked with citation references: TiC [19], VC [21], V(C,N) [19] (two points), V₄C₃ [20], NbC [19], and VC/Fe₃C [5], showing good agreement with the model predictions. As stated in the caption and text, the calculation employs the Ashby-Orowan equation (Δσ_p = 0.847·M·(1.2/π)·G·b·√(3/(2π))·(f^0.5/D)·ln(D/(2b))), indicating that fine, high-volume-fraction Ti-, V-, and Nb-containing carbides act as effective obstacles to dislocation sliding, while κ-carbides are sheared by dislocations and cannot provide continuous strengthening. This figure supports the design strategy of precipitation hardening in low-density steels.

Figure 8 presents a comprehensive investigation of wear behavior in Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steels, combining quantitative wear rate measurements with detailed SEM microstructural characterization. Panel (a) plots wear rate (×10³ mm³/Nm) versus aging time (1–3 h) for Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al steel aged at 500°C, 550°C, and 600°C, showing linear decreases with increasing aging time and temperature (R²=0.93–0.99), with the as-homogenized condition exhibiting the highest wear rate (~0.36×10³ mm³/Nm). Panels (b) and (c) display SEM images of worn surfaces and debris from the 600°C/3h aged specimen, revealing crater formation on the wear track (100 μm scale) and fragmented debris morphology (10 μm inset), indicating good wear resistance. In contrast, panels (d) and (e) examine Fe-28Mn-1C-8.5Al steel after overaging at 550°C for 4h (following 1050°C quenching), showing severe delamination (d) and microcracking propagation along broken coarse κ-carbide particles (e), with (Fe,Mn)₃AlC phases visible. These results demonstrate that while proper aging reduces wear rate through incoherent κ-carbide precipitation that inhibits crack nucleation, overaging causes κ-coarsening along grain boundaries, initiating cracks and delamination that deteriorate tribological performance.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructure of Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) low-density steel processed by metal injection molding (MIM) and sintered at 1360 °C. Subfigure (a) displays the sample sintered for 1 hour, while subfigure (b) shows the sample sintered for 3 hours. Both images reveal a fully austenitic matrix characterized by polygonal grains with visible annealing twins, alongside dark manganese oxide particles distributed both at grain boundaries and within grains. The scale bar indicates 200 μm. The micrographs demonstrate that the MIM process produces a fully austenitic structure with uniformly distributed manganese oxide particles, regardless of sintering duration within the examined range. These manganese oxide precipitates appear as black contrasting phases against the gray austenitic matrix. The images support the conclusion that MIM fabrication can achieve homogeneous microstructures in Fe–Mn–C–Al steels, which is crucial for enhancing wear-resistant properties through subsequent composite reinforcement with ultrahard particles such as SiC or alumina.

This figure presents a schematic roadmap illustrating the development pathway of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel, structured as a sequential three-stage process flow connected by a dashed pathway. The first stage, "Alloyings," focuses on controlling steel density, precipitation behavior, and strengthening mechanisms through compositional design with Mn, Al, and C additions. The second stage, "Annealing & aging treatments," involves critical thermal processing steps to induce phase changes and precipitation growth (particularly κ-carbide formation). The final stage yields the target "Low density steel" exhibiting four key performance attributes: lower density, higher strength, wear resistance, and low material cost. This visualization encapsulates the processing-microstructure-property paradigm central to the review, demonstrating how specific alloying strategies and controlled heat treatments translate Fe-Mn-C-Al alloys into lightweight structural materials with superior mechanical performance for automotive and aerospace applications.
